paragraphs.body: One brisk, fall morning in 2012, I left my house after making sure all my stuffed animals were lined up ready for me to come home at the end of the day, with my favorite one in the center, and got in the car to go to school. I stepped into my first-grade classroom beyond excited to learn, as usual. My teacher, Ms. Arluna was there with her comforting presence and had everyone go sit on the rug that had colors, shapes, and the alphabet. We all sat and listened to her read a story to start the day. We went about the day and did spelling, reading, gym, art, snack time, recess, lunch, and every other activity you’d expect to see in a common first- grade classroom. I knew the day was nearing an end because it was finally time for math. Math was always the last subject of the day, so I knew what to expect. I was so excited because we were learning fractions for the very first time and I was feeling fairly confident. I had my drawing of a pizza pie to help me understand better and was ready to answer my exit question. An exit question was a daily question about something we learned that day, and in order to leave for the day, you had to answer it correctly. The fraction related question was given to me on a small slip of paper, and I felt as though I knew exactly what to answer. I handed my slip to Ms. Arluna and she told me it was wrong and to try again. I wasn’t too worried because I usually got it within three tries. I repeatedly handed in my slip of paper, and it was handed right back to me over and over again. As I watched my classmates start getting it correct, grabbing their backpacks, and leaving for the day, I was getting more frustrated and really losing patience. What felt like an eternity had passed and I was still in the classroom with Ms. Arluna trying to solve the nearly impossible fraction problem. I slowly realized I was the last kid in the classroom and my grandma was patiently outside waiting for me to finish up. I was overwhelmed and for some reason panicking over not solving this problem, which was not like me. My teacher eventually helped me solve the fraction and let me leave, but I was still distraught the whole car ride home. I didn’t feel any sense of relief until I walked into my bedroom and saw Rosie, my stuffed, pink teddy bear sitting on my bed waiting for me in the center of my bed just as I left her. It was almost like she was just waiting for me and it felt like she knew I was going to need her more than ever when I got home. I didn’t let go of Rosie for the rest of the day and fell asleep knowing Rosie was right by my side. Rosie was my comfort object for my entire life and always will be.

Rosie, the pink, raggedy, piece of home I’ve always known was reminiscent of an old, reliable pair of ballet shoes. She always radiated warmth and comfort with her little ears partially ripped off and the discoloration of her light pink fur, that was once hot pink. Rosie was always found sitting on my soft, white pillow emitting nostalgia time and time again. Her presence is like a cozy blanket and hot cocoa on a snow day in elementary school. Her black, round eyes glimmered when the harsh, yellow, overhead light turned on. Her plush, delicate body was there without fail to listen to me after a dreadful, good, eternal, or short day. When I try to fall asleep, her familiar scent transports me back to a place of familiarity. Rosie’s small, stitched nose and subtle smile nestled into my neck at bedtime and reminded me of each time she has comforted me in the past. She has black paw pads that look like they’ve been at work for years, but still have a gentle touch to them. Rosie is more than a stuffed teddy bear. She is innocence, warmth, and love, along with being the protector of my dream's night after night. Dressed for every occasion like your first-grade teacher during the holiday season, Rosie was always my biggest supporter. As my tired eyes close each night, I feel her by my side and all my worries seem to disappear until the next day where I’m sure to find Rosie right where I left her. Rosie was always there for me when I needed her or was losing patience.

The word patience comes from Old French and Latin. It was originally used to describe the quality of being willing to suffer. In Old French “pacience” means sufferance or permission. In Latin “patientia” means the quality of suffering or enduring. It was used to describe calmness when waiting for something to eventually happen. Patience is an ability or willingness to suppress restlessness or annoyance when confronted with delay. Patience is learning a new concept in school that you don’t understand on the first try. Patience is waiting in an extensive line at the grocery store when all you want is to be at home. It is the stillness that exists while actively waiting for your best friend to come out of their house after texting them “here.” Patience is not an absence of endeavors, but it is a skill not all people can successfully act on. Patience often leads to frustration and how that frustration may cause you to behave. Ambitious young people do not tend to have natural patience, but it is something learned along their journey. Patience is equivalent to the phrase “quality over quantity.” Patience is not avoidance and therefore, patience is not comparable to procrastination. It is waiting for the day you reunite with your hometown best friends right as you need them the most. Someone who is not patient doesn’t know how to tolerate discomfort. Getting closer with a new friend day by day requires patience. Patience is subtle, but still powerful enough to be noticeable. It is waiting for the news you’ve been anticipating for weeks while nervously squeezing your comfort object.

Patience isn’t something that everyone is naturally born with. Some people are born naturally patient, but others have to really focus and work towards it. Some people need extra help exhibiting the quality, while others seem to pick it up on their own. Some people need a comfort object that helps motivate them to reach whatever goal they’re working at. Rosie has been a constant companion throughout the entirety of my life in times of uncertainty and is a reminder of how patience requires endurance. Rosie has always been the listener while I have always been the talker. I would always tell her about everything going on in my life, and she would always stay and listen. Rosie is one of the only constants in my life and this behavior clearly mirrors the virtue of patience.
